Hold, hurt him not, for God’s sake. He is mad.—
Some get within him; take his sword away.
Bind dromio too, and bear them to my house!

units.speaker: DROMIO OF SYRACUSE
units.text:
Run, master, run. For God’s sake, take a house.
This is some priory. In, or we are spoiled.
Exeunt ANTIPHOLUS OF SYRACUSE and DROMIO OF SYRACUSE to the priory
Enter the Lady ABBESS

units.speaker: ABBESS
units.text: Be quiet, people. Wherefore throng you hither?

units.speaker: ADRIANA
units.text:
To fetch my poor distracted husband hence.
Let us come in, that we may bind him fast
And bear him home for his recovery.

units.speaker: ANGELO
units.text: I knew he was not in his perfect wits.

units.speaker: SECOND MERCHANT
units.text: I am sorry now that I did draw on him.

units.speaker: ABBESS
units.text: How long hath this possession held the man?

units.speaker: ADRIANA
units.text:
This week he hath been heavy, sour, sad,
And much different from the man he was.
But till this afternoon his passion
Ne'er brake into extremity of rage.

units.speaker: ABBESS
units.text:
Hath he not lost much wealth by wrack of sea?
Buried some dear friend? Hath not else his eye
Stray’d his affection in unlawful love,
A sin prevailing much in youthful men
Who give their eyes the liberty of gazing?
Which of these sorrows is he subject to?

units.speaker: ADRIANA
units.text:
To none of these, except it be the last,
Namely, some love that drew him oft from home.

units.speaker: ABBESS
units.text: You should for that have reprehended him.

units.speaker: ADRIANA
units.text: Why, so I did.

units.speaker: ABBESS
units.text: Ay, but not rough enough.

units.speaker: ADRIANA
units.text: As roughly as my modesty would let me.

units.speaker: ABBESS
units.text: Haply in private.

units.speaker: ADRIANA
units.text: And in assemblies too.

units.speaker: ABBESS
units.text: Ay, but not enough.

units.speaker: ADRIANA
ABBESS
units.text:
And thereof came it that the man was mad.
The venom clamors of a jealous woman
Poisons more deadly than a mad dog’s tooth.
It seems his sleeps were hinder’d by thy railing,
And therefore comes it that his head is light.
Thou sayst his meat was sauced with thy upbraidings.
Unquiet meals make ill digestions.
Thereof the raging fire of fever bred,
And what’s a fever but a fit of madness?
Thou sayest his sports were hinderd by thy brawls.
Sweet recreation barred, what doth ensue
But moody and dull melancholy,
Kinsman to grim and comfortless despair,
And at her heels a huge infectious troop
Of pale distemperatures and foes to life?
In food, in sport, and life-preserving rest
To be disturbed, would mad or man or beast.
The consequence is, then, thy jealous fits
Have scared thy husband from the use of wits.

units.speaker: LUCIANA
units.text:
She never reprehended him but mildly
When he demeaned himself rough, rude, and wildly.—
[to ADRIANA] Why bear you these rebukes and answer not?

units.speaker: ADRIANA
units.text:
She did betray me to my own reproof.
Good people, enter and lay hold on him.

units.speaker: ABBESS
units.text: No, not a creature enters in my house.

units.speaker: ADRIANA
units.text: Then let your servants bring my husband forth.

units.speaker: ABBESS
units.text:
Neither: he took this place for sanctuary,
And it shall privilege him from your hands
Till I have brought him to his wits again
Or lose my labor in assaying it.

units.speaker: ADRIANA
units.text:
I will attend my husband, be his nurse,
Diet his sickness, for it is my office
And will have no attorney but myself;
And therefore let me have him home with me.

units.speaker: ABBESS
units.text:
Be patient, for I will not let him stir
Till I have used the approvd means I have,
With wholesome syrups, drugs, and holy prayers,
To make of him a formal man again.
It is a branch and parcel of mine oath,
A charitable duty of my order.
Therefore depart and leave him here with me.

units.speaker: ADRIANA
ABBESS
units.text: Be quiet and depart. Thou shalt not have him.
Exit ABBESS

units.speaker: LUCIANA
units.text: Complain unto the Duke of this indignity.

units.speaker: ADRIANA
units.text:
Come, go. I will fall prostrate at his feet
And never rise until my tears and prayers
Have won his grace to come in person hither
And take perforce my husband from the Abbess.

units.speaker: SECOND MERCHANT
units.text:
By this, I think, the dial points at five.
Anon, I’m sure, the Duke himself in person
Comes this way to the melancholy vale,
The place of death and sorry execution
Behind the ditches of the abbey here.

units.speaker: ANGELO
units.text: Upon what cause?

units.speaker: SECOND MERCHANT
units.text:
To see a reverend Syracusian merchant,
Who put unluckily into this bay
Against the laws and statutes of this town,
Beheaded publicly for his offense.

units.speaker: ANGELO
units.text: See where they come. We will behold his death.

units.speaker: LUCIANA
units.text: Kneel to the duke before he pass the abbey.
Enter the DUKE OF EPHESUS and EGEON the merchant of Syracuse, bare head, with the headsman and other officers

units.speaker: DUKE
units.text:
Yet once again proclaim it publicly,
If any friend will pay the sum for him,
He shall not die; so much we tender him.

units.speaker: ADRIANA
units.text: Justice, most sacred duke, against the Abbess.

units.speaker: DUKE
units.text:
She is a virtuous and a reverend lady.
It cannot be that she hath done thee wrong.

units.speaker: ADRIANA
units.text:
May it please your Grace, Antipholus my husband,
Whom I made lord of me and all I had
At your important letters, this ill day
A most outrageous fit of madness took him,
That desp'rately he hurried through the street,
With him his bondman, all as mad as he,
Doing displeasure to the citizens
By rushing in their houses, bearing thence
Rings, jewels, any thing his rage did like.
Once did I get him bound and sent him home
Whilst to take order for the wrongs I went
That here and there his fury had committed.
Anon, I wot not by what strong escape,
He broke from those that had the guard of him,
And with his mad attendant and himself,
Each one with ireful passion, with drawn swords,
Met us again and, madly bent on us,
Chased us away, till, raising of more aid,
We came again to bind them. Then they fled
Into this abbey, whither we pursued them,
And here the Abbess shuts the gates on us
And will not suffer us to fetch him out,
Nor send him forth that we may bear him hence.
Therefore, most gracious duke, with thy command
Let him be brought forth and borne hence for help.

units.speaker: DUKE
units.text:
Long since thy husband served me in my wars,
And I to thee engaged a prince’s word,
When thou didst make him master of thy bed,
To do him all the grace and good I could.
Go, some of you, knock at the abbey gate,
And bid the Lady Abbess come to me.
I will determine this before I stir.
Enter a MESSENGER

units.speaker: MESSENGER
units.text:
O mistress, mistress, shift and save yourself.
My master and his man are both broke loose,
Beaten the maids a-row, and bound the doctor,
Whose beard they have singed off with brands of fire,
And ever as it blazed, they threw on him
Great pails of puddled mire to quench the hair.
My master preaches patience to him, and the while
His man with scissors nicks him like a fool;
And sure, unless you send some present help,
Between them they will kill the conjurer.

units.speaker: ADRIANA
units.text:
Peace, fool. Thy master and his man are here,
And that is false thou dost report to us.

units.speaker: MESSENGER
units.text:
Mistress, upon my life I tell you true.
I have not breathed almost since I did see it.
He cries for you, and vows, if he can take you,
To scorch your face and to disfigure you.

units.speaker: DUKE
units.text: Come, stand by me. Fear nothing.—Guard with halberds.
Enter ANTIPHOLUS OF EPHESUS and DROMIO OF EPHESUS

units.speaker: ADRIANA
units.text:
Ay me, it is my husband. Witness you
That he is borne about invisible.
Even now we housed him in the abbey here,
And now he’s there, past thought of human reason.

units.speaker: ANTIPHOLUS OF EPHESUS
units.text:
Justice, most gracious duke, O, grant me justice,
Even for the service that long since I did thee
When I bestrid thee in the wars and took
Deep scars to save thy life. Even for the blood
That then I lost for thee, now grant me justice.

units.speaker: EGEON
units.text:
[aside] Unless the fear of death doth make me dote,
I see my son Antipholus and Dromio.

units.speaker: ANTIPHOLUS OF EPHESUS
units.text:
Justice, sweet prince, against that woman there,
She whom thou gav’st to me to be my wife,
That hath abusèd and dishonored me
Even in the strength and height of injury.
Beyond imagination is the wrong
That she this day hath shameless thrown on me.

units.speaker: DUKE
units.text: Discover how, and thou shalt find me just.

units.speaker: ANTIPHOLUS OF EPHESUS
units.text:
This day, great duke, she shut the doors upon me
While she with harlots feasted in my house.

units.speaker: DUKE
units.text: A grievous fault.—Say, woman, didst thou so?

units.speaker: ADRIANA
units.text:
No, my good lord. Myself, he, and my sister
Today did dine together. So befall my soul
As this is false he burdens me withal.

units.speaker: LUCIANA
units.text:
Ne'er may I look on day, nor sleep on night
But she tells to your Highness simple truth.

units.speaker: ANGELO
units.text:
O perjured woman! —They are both forsworn.
In this the madman justly chargeth them.

units.speaker: ANTIPHOLUS OF EPHESUS
units.text:
My liege, I am advisèd what I say,
Neither disturbed with the effect of wine,
Nor heady-rash, provoked with raging ire,
Albeit my wrongs might make one wiser mad.
This woman locked me out this day from dinner.
That goldsmith there, were he not packed with her,
Could witness it, for he was with me then,
Who parted with me to go fetch a chain,
Promising to bring it to the Porpentine,
Where Balthasar and I did dine together.
Our dinner done and he not coming thither,
I went to seek him. In the street I met him,
And in his company that gentleman. [points to the
SECOND MERCHANT ]
There did this perjured goldsmith swear me down
That I this day of him received the chain,
Which, God he knows, I saw not; for the which
He did arrest me with an officer.
I did obey, and sent my peasant home
For certain ducats. He with none returned.
Then fairly I bespoke the officer
To go in person with me to my house.
By th' way we met
My wife, her sister, and a rabble more
Of vile confederates. Along with them
They brought one Pinch, a hungry, lean-faced villain,
A mere anatomy, a mountebank,
A threadbare juggler, and a fortune-teller,
A needy, hollow-eyed, sharp-looking wretch,
A living dead man. This pernicious slave,
Forsooth, took on him as a conjurer,
And, gazing in mine eyes, feeling my pulse,
And with no face (as ’twere) outfacing me,
Cries out I was possessed. Then all together
They fell upon me, bound me, bore me thence,
And in a dark and dankish vault at home
There left me and my man, both bound together,
Till gnawing with my teeth my bonds in sunder,
I gained my freedom and immediately
Ran hither to your Grace, whom I beseech
To give me ample satisfaction
For these deep shames and great indignities.

units.speaker: ANGELO
units.text:
My lord, in truth, thus far I witness with him:
That he dined not at home, but was locked out.

units.speaker: DUKE
units.text: But had he such a chain of thee or no?

units.speaker: ANGELO
units.text:
He had, my lord, and when he ran in here,
These people saw the chain about his neck.

units.speaker: SECOND MERCHANT
units.text:
Besides, I will be sworn these ears of mine
Heard you confess you had the chain of him
After you first forswore it on the mart,
And thereupon I drew my sword on you,
And then you fled into this abbey here,
From whence I think you are come by miracle.

units.speaker: ANTIPHOLUS OF EPHESUS
units.text:
I never came within these abbey walls,
Nor ever didst thou draw thy sword on me.
I never saw the chain, so help me heaven,
And this is false you burden me withal.

units.speaker: DUKE
units.text:
Why, what an intricate impeach is this!
I think you all have drunk of Circe’s cup.
If here you housed him, here he would have been.
If he were mad, he would not plead so coldly.
[to ADRIANA ] You say he dined at home; the goldsmith
here
Denies that saying. Sirrah, what say you?

units.speaker: DROMIO OF EPHESUS
units.text: Sir, he dined with her there, at the Porpentine.

units.speaker: COURTESAN
units.text: He did, and from my finger snatched that ring.

units.speaker: ANTIPHOLUS OF EPHESUS
units.text: 'Tis true, my liege; this ring I had of her.

units.speaker: DUKE
units.text: Saw’st thou him enter at the abbey here?

units.speaker: COURTESAN
units.text: As sure, my liege, as I do see your Grace.

units.speaker: DUKE
units.text:
Why, this is strange.—Go call the Abbess hither.
I think you are all mated or stark mad.
Exit one to ABBESS

units.speaker: EGEON
units.text:
Most mighty duke, vouchsafe me speak a word.
Haply I see a friend will save my life
And pay the sum that may deliver me.

units.speaker: DUKE
units.text: Speak freely, Syracusian, what thou wilt.

units.speaker: EGEON
units.text:
[to ANTIPHOLUS OF EPHESUS ] Is not your name, sir,
called
Antipholus?
And is not that your bondman Dromio?

Within this hour I was his bondman sir,
But he, I thank him, gnawed in two my cords.
Now am I Dromio, and his man, unbound.

units.speaker: EGEON
units.text: I am sure you both of you remember me.

units.speaker: DROMIO OF EPHESUS
units.text:
Ourselves we do remember, sir, by you.
For lately we were bound as you are now.
You are not Pinch’s patient, are you, sir?

units.speaker: EGEON
units.text: Why look you strange on me? you know me well.

units.speaker: ANTIPHOLUS OF EPHESUS
units.text: I never saw you in my life till now.

units.speaker: EGEON
units.text:
O, grief hath changed me since you saw me last,
And careful hours with time’s deformèd hand
Have written strange defeatures in my face.
But tell me yet, dost thou not know my voice?

units.speaker: ANTIPHOLUS OF EPHESUS
units.text: Neither.

units.speaker: EGEON
units.text: Dromio, nor thou?

units.speaker: DROMIO OF EPHESUS
units.text: No, trust me, sir, nor I.

units.speaker: EGEON
units.text: I am sure thou dost.

units.speaker: DROMIO OF EPHESUS
units.text:
Ay, sir, but I am sure I do not, and whatsoever a man
denies, you are now bound to believe him.

units.speaker: EGEON
units.text:
Not know my voice! O time’s extremity,
Hast thou so crack’d and splitted my poor tongue
In seven short years that here my only son
Knows not my feeble key of untuned cares?
Though now this grainèd face of mine be hid
In sap-consuming winter’s drizzled snow,
And all the conduits of my blood froze up,
Yet hath my night of life some memory,
My wasting lamps some fading glimmer left,
My dull deaf ears a little use to hear.
All these old witnesses—I cannot err—
Tell me thou art my son Antipholus.

units.speaker: ANTIPHOLUS OF EPHESUS
units.text: I never saw my father in my life.

units.speaker: EGEON
units.text:
But seven years since, in Syracusa, boy,
Thou know’st we parted. But perhaps, my son,
Thou sham’st to acknowledge me in misery.

units.speaker: ANTIPHOLUS OF EPHESUS
units.text:
The Duke and all that know me in the city
Can witness with me that it is not so
I ne'er saw Syracusa in my life.

units.speaker: DUKE
units.text:
I tell thee, Syracusian, twenty years
Have I been patron to Antipholus,
During which time he ne'er saw Syracusa.
I see thy age and dangers make thee dote.
Enter the ABBESS with ANTIPHOLUS OF SYRACUSE and DROMIO OF SYRACUSE

units.speaker: ABBESS
units.text: Most mighty duke, behold a man much wronged.

units.speaker: ADRIANA
units.text: I see two husbands, or mine eyes deceive me.

units.speaker: DUKE
units.text:
[looks at the ANTIPHOLUS tw ins] One of these men is
genius to the other.
[looks at the DROMIO tw ins] And so, of these, which is
the natural man
And which the spirit? Who deciphers them?

units.speaker: DROMIO OF SYRACUSE
units.text: I, sir, am Dromio. Command him away.

units.speaker: DROMIO OF EPHESUS
units.text: I, sir, am Dromio. Please, let me stay.

units.speaker: ANTIPHOLUS OF SYRACUSE
units.text: Egeon art thou not, or else his ghost?

units.speaker: DROMIO OF SYRACUSE
units.text: O, my old master.—Who hath bound him here?

units.speaker: ABBESS
units.text:
Whoever bound him, I will loose his bonds
And gain a husband by his liberty.—
Speak, old Egeon, if thou be’st the man
That hadst a wife once called Emilia,
That bore thee at a burden two fair sons.
O, if thou be’st the same Egeon, speak,
And speak unto the same Emilia.

units.speaker: DUKE
units.text:
Why, here begins his morning story right;
These two Antipholuses, these two so like,
And these two Dromios, one in semblance—
Besides his urging of her wreck at sea—
These are the parents to these children,
Which accidentally are met together.

units.speaker: EGEON
units.text:
If I dream not, thou art Emilia.
If thou art she, tell me where is that son
That floated with thee on the fatal raft?

units.speaker: ABBESS
units.text:
By men of Epidamnum he and I
And the twin Dromio all were taken up;
But by and by rude fishermen of Corinth
By force took Dromio and my son from them
And me they left with those of Epidamnum.
What then became of them I cannot tell;
I to this fortune that you see me in.

units.speaker: DUKE
units.text:
[to ANTIPHOLUS OF SYRACUSE ] Antipholus, thou cam’st
from
Corinth first.

units.speaker: ANTIPHOLUS OF SYRACUSE
units.text: No, sir, not I. I came from Syracuse.

units.speaker: DUKE
units.text: Stay, stand apart. I know not which is which.

units.speaker: ANTIPHOLUS OF EPHESUS
units.text: I came from Corinth, my most gracious lord.

units.speaker: DROMIO OF EPHESUS
units.text: And I with him.

units.speaker: ANTIPHOLUS OF EPHESUS
units.text:
Brought to this town by that most famous warrior
Duke Menaphon, your most renownèd uncle.

units.speaker: ADRIANA
units.text: Which of you two did dine with me today?

units.speaker: ANTIPHOLUS OF SYRACUSE
units.text: I, gentle mistress.

units.speaker: ADRIANA
units.text: And are not you my husband?

units.speaker: ANTIPHOLUS OF EPHESUS
units.text: No, I say nay to that.

units.speaker: ANTIPHOLUS OF SYRACUSE
units.text:
And so do I, yet did she call me so,
And this fair gentlewoman, her sister here,
Did call me brother. [t o LUCIANA ] What I told you then
I hope I shall have leisure to make good,
If this be not a dream I see and hear.

units.speaker: ANGELO
units.text: That is the chain, sir, which you had of me.

units.speaker: ANTIPHOLUS OF SYRACUSE
units.text: I think it be, sir. I deny it not.

units.speaker: ANTIPHOLUS OF EPHESUS
units.text: And you, sir, for this chain arrested me.

units.speaker: ANGELO
units.text: I think I did, sir. I deny it not.

units.speaker: ADRIANA
units.text:
I sent you money, sir, to be your bail
By Dromio, but I think he brought it not.

units.speaker: DROMIO OF EPHESUS
units.text: No, none by me.

units.speaker: ANTIPHOLUS OF SYRACUSE
units.text:
This purse of ducats I received from you,
And Dromio my man did bring them me.
I see we still did meet each other’s man,
And I was ta'en for him, and he for me,
And thereupon these errors are arose.

units.speaker: ANTIPHOLUS OF EPHESUS
units.text: These ducats pawn I for my father here.

units.speaker: DUKE
units.text: It shall not need. Thy father hath his life.

units.speaker: COURTESAN
units.text: Sir, I must have that diamond from you.

units.speaker: ANTIPHOLUS OF EPHESUS
units.text: There, take it; and much thanks for my good cheer.

units.speaker: ABBESS
units.text:
Renownèd duke, vouchsafe to take the pains
To go with us into the abbey here
And hear at large discoursed all our fortunes,
And all that are assembled in this place
That by this sympathizèd one day’s error
Have suffered wrong. Go, keep us company,
And we shall make full satisfaction.—
Thirty-three years have I but gone in travail
Of you, my sons, and till this present hour
My heavy burden ne'er deliverèd.—
The Duke, my husband, and my children both,
And you, the calendars of their nativity,
Go to a gossips' feast, and go with me.
After so long grief, such nativity!

units.speaker: DUKE
units.text: With all my heart I’ll gossip at this feast.
Exeunt; the two DROMIOS and the two ANTIPHOLUS brothers remain behind.

units.speaker: DROMIO OF SYRACUSE
units.text:
[to ANTIPHOLUS OF EPHESUS ] Master, shall I fetch your
stuff from shipboard?

units.speaker: ANTIPHOLUS OF EPHESUS
units.text: Dromio, what stuff of mine hast thou embarked?

units.speaker: DROMIO OF SYRACUSE
units.text: Your goods that lay at host, sir, in the Centaur.

units.speaker: ANTIPHOLUS OF SYRACUSE
units.text:
He speaks to me.—I am your master, Dromio.
Come, go with us. We’ll look to that anon.
Embrace thy brother there. Rejoice with him.
Exeunt ANTIPHOLUS OF SYRACUSE and ANTIPHOLUS OF EPHESUS

units.speaker: DROMIO OF SYRACUSE
units.text:
There is a fat friend at your master’s house
That kitchened me for you today at dinner.
She now shall be my sister, not my wife.

units.speaker: DROMIO OF EPHESUS
units.text:
Methinks you are my glass, and not my brother:
I see by you I am a sweet-faced youth.
Will you walk in to see their gossiping?

units.speaker: DROMIO OF SYRACUSE
units.text: Not I, sir. You are my elder.

units.speaker: DROMIO OF EPHESUS
units.text: That’s a question. How shall we try it?

units.speaker: DROMIO OF SYRACUSE
units.text:
We’ll draw cuts for the signior. Till then, lead thou
first.

units.speaker: DROMIO OF EPHESUS
units.text:
Nay, then, thus:
We came into the world like brother and brother,
And now let’s go hand in hand, not one before another.
Exeunt

sentences.text: Enter the DUKE, EGEON, JAILER, and other attendants

sentences.speaker: EGEON
sentences.text:
Proceed, Solinus, to procure my fall,
And by the doom of death end woes and all.

sentences.speaker: EGEON
sentences.text:
Proceed, Solinus, to procure my fall,
And by the doom of death end woes and all.

sentences.speaker: DUKE
sentences.text:
Merchant of Syracuse, plead no more.
I am not partial to infringe our laws.
The enmity and discord which of late
Sprung from the rancorous outrage of your duke
To merchants, our well-dealing countrymen,
Who, wanting guilders to redeem their lives,
Have seal’d his rigorous statutes with their bloods,
Excludes all pity from our threatening looks.
For, since the mortal and intestine jars
'Twixt thy seditious countrymen and us,
It hath in solemn synods been decreed
Both by the Syracusians and ourselves,
To admit no traffic to our adverse towns.
Nay, more, if any born at Ephesus
Be seen at any Syracusian marts and fairs;
Again, if any Syracusian born
Come to the bay of Ephesus, he dies,
His goods confiscate to the Duke’s dispose,
Unless a thousand marks be levièd
To quit the penalty and to ransom him.
Thy substance, valued at the highest rate,
Cannot amount unto a hundred marks;
Therefore by law thou art condemned to die.

sentences.speaker: EGEON
sentences.text:
Yet this my comfort: when your words are done,
My woes end likewise with the evening sun.

sentences.speaker: DUKE
sentences.text:
Well, Syracusian, say in brief the cause
Why thou dep-artedst from thy native home
And for what cause thou camest to Ephesus.

sentences.speaker: EGEON
sentences.text:
A heavier task could not have been imposed
Than I to speak my griefs unspeakable;
Yet, that the world may witness that my end
Was wrought by nature, not by vile offense,
I’ll utter what my sorrow gives me leave.
In Syracusa was I born, and wed
Unto a woman happy but for me,
And by me, had not our hap been bad.
With her I lived in joy. Our wealth increased
By prosperous voyages I often made
To Epidamnum, till my factor’s death
And the great care of goods at random left
Drew me from kind embracements of my spouse;
From whom my absence was not six months old
Before herself— almost at fainting under
The pleasing punishment that women bear—
Had made provision for her following me
And soon and safe arrivèd where I was.
There had she not been long but she became
A joyful mother of two goodly sons,
And, which was strange, the one so like the other
As could not be distinguished but by names.
That very hour, and in the selfsame inn,
A meaner woman was deliverèd
Of such a burden, male twins, both alike.
Those, for their parents were exceeding poor,
I bought and brought up to attend my sons.
My wife, not meanly proud of two such boys,
Made daily motions for our home return.
Unwilling, I agreed. Alas, too soon
We came aboard.
A league from Epidamnum had we sailed
Before the always-wind-obeying deep
Gave any tragic instance of our harm;
But longer did we not retain much hope;
For what obscured light the heavens did grant
Did but convey unto our fearful minds
A doubtful warrant of immediate death,
Which though myself would gladly have embraced,
Yet the incessant weepings of my wife,
Weeping before for what she saw must come,
And piteous plainings of the pretty babes,
That mourned for fashion, ignorant what to fear,
Forced me to seek delays for them and me.
And this it was, for other means was none:
The sailors sought for safety by our boat
And left the ship, then sinking-ripe, to us.
My wife, more careful for the latter-born,
Had fastened him unto a small spare mast,
Such as seafaring men provide for storms.
To him one of the other twins was bound,
Whilst I had been like heedful of the other.
The children thus disposed, my wife and I,
Fixing our eyes on whom our care was fixed,
Fastened ourselves at either end the mast
And, floating straight, obedient to the stream,
Was carried towards Corinth, as we thought.
At length the sun, gazing upon the earth,
Dispersed those vapors that offended us,
And by the benefit of his wished light
The seas waxed calm, and we discoverèd
Two ships from far, making amain to us,
Of Corinth that, of Epidaurus this.
But ere they came,— O, let me say no more!
Gather the sequel by that went before.

Those, for their parents were exceeding poor,

Made daily motions for our home return.

We came aboard.

Gave any tragic instance of our harm;

A doubtful warrant of immediate death,
Which though myself would gladly have embraced,
Yet the incessant weepings of my wife,
Weeping before for what she saw must come,
And piteous plainings of the pretty babes,
That mourned for fashion, ignorant what to fear,
Forced me to seek delays for them and me.
And this it was, for other means was none:
The sailors sought for safety by our boat
And left the ship, then sinking-ripe, to us.
My wife, more careful for the latter-born,
Had fastened him unto a small spare mast,
Such as seafaring men provide for storms.
To him one of the other twins was bound,
Whilst I had been like heedful of the other.
The children thus disposed, my wife and I,
Fixing our eyes on whom our care was fixed,
Fastened ourselves at either end the mast
And, floating straight, obedient to the stream,
Was carried towards Corinth, as we thought.

Dispersed those vapors that offended us,
And by the benefit of his wished light
The seas waxed calm, and we discoverèd

Of Corinth that, of Epidaurus this.
But ere they came,— O, let me say no more!
Gather the sequel by that went before.

sentences.speaker: DUKE
sentences.text:
Nay, forward, old man. Do not break off so,
For we may pity though not pardon thee.

sentences.speaker: DUKE
sentences.text:
Nay, forward, old man. Do not break off so,
For we may pity though not pardon thee.

sentences.speaker: EGEON
sentences.text:
O, had the gods done so, I had not now
Worthily termed them merciless to us.
For, ere the ships could meet by twice five leagues,
We were encountered by a mighty rock,
Which being violently borne upon,
Our helpful ship was splitted in the midst;
So that, in this unjust divorce of us,
Fortune had left to both of us alike
What to delight in, what to sorrow for.
Her part, poor soul, seeming as burdenèd
With lesser weight, but not with lesser woe,
Was carried with more speed before the wind,
And in our sight they three were taken up
By fishermen of Corinth, as we thought.
At length, another ship had seized on us
And, knowing whom it was their hap to save,
Gave healthful welcome to their shipwracked guests,
And would have reft the fishers of their prey
Had not their bark been very slow of sail;
And therefore homeward did they bend their course.
Thus have you heard me severed from my bliss;
That by misfortunes was my life prolonged
To tell sad stories of my own mishaps.

And in our sight they three were taken up
By fishermen of Corinth, as we thought.
At length, another ship had seized on us
And, knowing whom it was their hap to save,
Gave healthful welcome to their shipwracked guests,
And would have reft the fishers of their prey
Had not their bark been very slow of sail;
And therefore homeward did they bend their course.
Thus have you heard me severed from my bliss;
That by misfortunes was my life prolonged
To tell sad stories of my own mishaps.

sentences.speaker: EGEON
sentences.text:
O, had the gods done so, I had not now
Worthily termed them merciless to us.
For, ere the ships could meet by twice five leagues,
We were encountered by a mighty rock,
Which being violently borne upon,
Our helpful ship was splitted in the midst;
So that, in this unjust divorce of us,
Fortune had left to both of us alike
What to delight in, what to sorrow for.
Her part, poor soul, seeming as burdenèd
With lesser weight, but not with lesser woe,
Was carried with more speed before the wind,
And in our sight they three were taken up
By fishermen of Corinth, as we thought.
At length, another ship had seized on us
And, knowing whom it was their hap to save,
Gave healthful welcome to their shipwracked guests,
And would have reft the fishers of their prey
Had not their bark been very slow of sail;
And therefore homeward did they bend their course.
Thus have you heard me severed from my bliss;
That by misfortunes was my life prolonged
To tell sad stories of my own mishaps.

sentences.speaker: DUKE
sentences.text:
And for the sake of them thou sorrowest for,
Do me the favour to dilate at full
What hath befall'n of them and thee till now.

sentences.speaker: EGEON
sentences.text:
My youngest boy, and yet my eldest care,
At eighteen years became inquisitive
After his brother, and importuned me
That his attendant— so his case was like,
Reft of his brother, but retained his name—
Might bear him company in the quest of him,
Whom whilst I laboured of a love to see,
I hazarded the loss of whom I loved.
Five summers have I spent in farthest Greece,
Roaming clean through the bounds of Asia,
And, coasting homeward, came to Ephesus,
Hopeless to find, yet loath to leave unsought
Or that or any place that harbors men.
But here must end the story of my life;
And happy were I in my timely death
Could all my travels warrant me they live.

sentences.speaker: DUKE
sentences.text:
Hapless Egeon, whom the fates have marked
To bear the extremity of dire mishap,
Now, trust me, were it not against our laws,
Against my crown, my oath, my dignity,
Which princes, would they, may not disannul,
My soul would sue as advocate for thee.
But though thou art adjudgèd to the death,
And passèd sentence may not be recalled
But to our honour’s great disparagement,
Yet will I favor thee in what I can.
Therefore, merchant, I’ll limit thee this day
To seek thy life by beneficial help.
Try all the friends thou hast in Ephesus;
Beg thou, or borrow, to make up the sum,
And live. If no, then thou art doom’d to die.—
Jailer, take him to thy custody.

sentences.speaker: JAILER
sentences.text: I will, my lord.

sentences.speaker: EGEON
sentences.text:
Hopeless and helpless doth Egeon wend,
But to procrastinate his lifeless end.
Exeunt
Enter ANTIPHOLUS OF SYRACUSE, DROMIO OF SYRACUSE, and FIRST MERCHANT

sentences.speaker: FIRST MERCHANT
sentences.text:
Therefore give out you are of Epidamnum,
Lest that your goods too soon be confiscate.
This very day a Syracusian merchant
Is apprehended for arrival here
And, not being able to buy out his life,
According to the statute of the town
Dies ere the weary sun set in the west.
There is your money that I had to keep.

sentences.speaker: FIRST MERCHANT

Dies ere the weary sun set in the west.
There is your money that I had to keep.

sentences.speaker: ANTIPHOLUS OF SYRACUSE
sentences.text:
Go bear it to the Centaur, where we host,
And stay there, Dromio, till I come to thee.
Within this hour it will be dinnertime.
Till that, I’ll view the manners of the town,
Peruse the traders, gaze upon the buildings,
And then return and sleep within mine inn,
For with long travel I am stiff and weary.
Get thee away.

sentences.speaker: DROMIO OF SYRACUSE
sentences.text:
Many a man would take you at your word
And go indeed, having so good a mean.
Exit DROMIO OF SYRACUSE

sentences.speaker: ANTIPHOLUS OF SYRACUSE
sentences.text:
A trusty villain, sir, that very oft,
When I am dull with care and melancholy,
Lightens my humor with his merry jests.
What, will you walk with me about the town
And then go to my inn and dine with me?

sentences.speaker: ANTIPHOLUS OF SYRACUSE

sentences.speaker: FIRST MERCHANT
sentences.text:
I am invited, sir, to certain merchants,
Of whom I hope to make much benefit.
I crave your pardon. Soon at five o'clock,
Please you, I’ll meet with you upon the mart
And afterward consort you till bedtime.
My present business calls me from you now.

sentences.speaker: ANTIPHOLUS OF SYRACUSE
sentences.text:
Farewell till then. I will go lose myself
And wander up and down to view the city.

sentences.speaker: ANTIPHOLUS OF SYRACUSE
sentences.text:
Farewell till then. I will go lose myself
And wander up and down to view the city.

sentences.speaker: FIRST MERCHANT
sentences.text: Sir, I commend you to your own content.
Exit FIRST MERCHANT exits.

sentences.speaker: ANTIPHOLUS OF SYRACUSE
sentences.text:
He that commends me to mine own content
Commends me to the thing I cannot get.
I to the world am like a drop of water
That in the ocean seeks another drop,
Who, falling there to find his fellow forth,
Unseen, inquisitive, confounds himself.
So I, to find a mother and a brother,
In quest of them, unhappy, lose myself.

sentences.speaker: ANTIPHOLUS OF SYRACUSE
sentences.text:
Here comes the almanac of my true date.—
What now? How chance thou art returned so soon?

sentences.speaker: ANTIPHOLUS OF SYRACUSE

Here comes the almanac of my true date.—
What now? How chance thou art returned so soon?

sentences.speaker: DROMIO OF EPHESUS

ANTIPHOLUS OF SYRACUSE
sentences.text:
Stop in your wind, sir. Tell me this, I pray:
Where have you left the money that I gave you?

sentences.speaker: ANTIPHOLUS OF SYRACUSE
sentences.text:
Stop in your wind, sir. Tell me this, I pray:
Where have you left the money that I gave you?

sentences.speaker: DROMIO OF EPHESUS
sentences.text:
O, sixpence, that I had o' Wednesday last
To pay the saddler for my mistress' crupper?
The saddler had it, sir; I kept it not.

sentences.speaker: DROMIO OF EPHESUS

sentences.speaker: ANTIPHOLUS OF SYRACUSE
sentences.text:
I am not in a sportive humor now.
Tell me, and dally not: where is the money?
We being strangers here, how dar’st thou trust
So great a charge from thine own custody?

sentences.speaker: ANTIPHOLUS OF SYRACUSE

sentences.speaker: DROMIO OF EPHESUS
sentences.text:
I pray you, jest, sir, as you sit at dinner.
I from my mistress come to you in post;
If I return, I shall be post indeed,
For she will scour your fault upon my pate.
Methinks your maw, like mine, should be your clock,
And strike you home without a messenger.

sentences.speaker: ANTIPHOLUS OF SYRACUSE
sentences.text:
Come, Dromio, come, these jests are out of season.
Reserve them till a merrier hour than this.
Where is the gold I gave in charge to thee?

sentences.speaker: ANTIPHOLUS OF SYRACUSE
sentences.text:
Come, Dromio, come, these jests are out of season.
Reserve them till a merrier hour than this.
Where is the gold I gave in charge to thee?

sentences.speaker: ANTIPHOLUS OF SYRACUSE

sentences.speaker: DROMIO OF EPHESUS
sentences.text: To me, sir? Why, you gave no gold to me!

sentences.speaker: DROMIO OF EPHESUS
sentences.text: To me, sir? Why, you gave no gold to me!

sentences.speaker: ANTIPHOLUS OF SYRACUSE
sentences.text:
Come on, sir knave, have done your foolishness,
And tell me how thou hast disposed thy charge.

sentences.speaker: DROMIO OF EPHESUS
sentences.text:
My charge was but to fetch you from the mart
Home to your house, the Phoenix, sir, to dinner.
My mistress and her sister stays for you.

sentences.speaker: DROMIO OF EPHESUS
sentences.text:
My charge was but to fetch you from the mart
Home to your house, the Phoenix, sir, to dinner.
My mistress and her sister stays for you.

sentences.speaker: ANTIPHOLUS OF SYRACUSE
sentences.text:
Now, as I am a Christian, answer me
In what safe place you have bestowed my money,
Or I shall break that merry sconce of yours
That stands on tricks when I am undisposed.
Where is the thousand marks thou hadst of me?

sentences.speaker: ANTIPHOLUS OF SYRACUSE
sentences.text:
Now, as I am a Christian, answer me
In what safe place you have bestowed my money,
Or I shall break that merry sconce of yours
That stands on tricks when I am undisposed.
Where is the thousand marks thou hadst of me?

sentences.speaker: DROMIO OF EPHESUS
sentences.text:
I have some marks of yours upon my pate,
Some of my mistress' marks upon my shoulders,
But not a thousand marks between you both.
If I should pay your worship those again,
Perchance you will not bear them patiently.

sentences.speaker: DROMIO OF EPHESUS
sentences.text:
I have some marks of yours upon my pate,
Some of my mistress' marks upon my shoulders,
But not a thousand marks between you both.
If I should pay your worship those again,
Perchance you will not bear them patiently.

sentences.speaker: ANTIPHOLUS OF SYRACUSE
sentences.text: Thy mistress' marks? what mistress, slave, hast thou?

sentences.speaker: ANTIPHOLUS OF SYRACUSE
sentences.text: Thy mistress' marks? what mistress, slave, hast thou?

sentences.speaker: DROMIO OF EPHESUS
sentences.text:
Your worship’s wife, my mistress at the Phoenix,
She that doth fast till you come home to dinner
And prays that you will hie you home to dinner.

sentences.speaker: ANTIPHOLUS OF SYRACUSE
sentences.text:
What, wilt thou flout me thus unto my face,
Being forbid? There, take you that, sir knave. [beats
DROMIO OF EPHESUS]

sentences.speaker: ANTIPHOLUS OF SYRACUSE
sentences.text:
What, wilt thou flout me thus unto my face,
Being forbid? There, take you that, sir knave. [beats
DROMIO OF EPHESUS]

sentences.speaker: DROMIO OF EPHESUS
sentences.text:
What mean you, sir? For God’s sake, hold your hands.
Nay, an you will not, sir, I’ll take my heels.

sentences.speaker: DROMIO OF EPHESUS
sentences.text:
What mean you, sir? For God’s sake, hold your hands.
Nay, an you will not, sir, I’ll take my heels.

sentences.speaker: DROMIO OF EPHESUS
sentences.text:
What mean you, sir? For God’s sake, hold your hands.
Nay, an you will not, sir, I’ll take my heels.
Exit DROMIO OF EPHESUS

sentences.speaker: ANTIPHOLUS OF SYRACUSE
sentences.text:
Upon my life, by some device or other
The villain is o'erraught of all my money.
They say this town is full of cozenage,
As nimble jugglers that deceive the eye,
Dark-working sorcerers that change the mind,
Soul-killing witches that deform the body,
Disguised cheaters, prating mountebanks,
And many suchlike liberties of sin.
If it prove so, I will be gone the sooner.
I’ll to the Centaur to go seek this slave.
I greatly fear my money is not safe.

sentences.speaker: ANTIPHOLUS OF SYRACUSE
sentences.text:
Upon my life, by some device or other
The villain is o'erraught of all my money.
They say this town is full of cozenage,
As nimble jugglers that deceive the eye,
Dark-working sorcerers that change the mind,
Soul-killing witches that deform the body,
Disguised cheaters, prating mountebanks,
And many suchlike liberties of sin.
If it prove so, I will be gone the sooner.
I’ll to the Centaur to go seek this slave.
I greatly fear my money is not safe.

Enter ADRIANA and LUCIANA

sentences.speaker: ADRIANA
sentences.text:
Neither my husband nor the slave returned
That in such haste I sent to seek his master?
Sure, Luciana, it is two o'clock.

sentences.speaker: ADRIANA
sentences.text:
Neither my husband nor the slave returned
That in such haste I sent to seek his master?
Sure, Luciana, it is two o'clock.

sentences.speaker: LUCIANA
sentences.text:
Perhaps some merchant hath invited him,
And from the mart he’s somewhere gone to dinner.
Good sister, let us dine and never fret.
A man is master of his liberty;
Time is their master, and when they see time
They’ll go or come. If so, be patient, sister.

sentences.speaker: LUCIANA
sentences.text:

And from the mart he’s somewhere gone to dinner.
Good sister, let us dine and never fret.
A man is master of his liberty;
Time is their master, and when they see time
They’ll go or come. If so, be patient, sister.

sentences.speaker: ADRIANA
sentences.text: Why should their liberty than ours be more?

sentences.speaker: LUCIANA
sentences.text: Because their business still lies out o' door.

sentences.speaker: ADRIANA
sentences.text: Look when I serve him so, he takes it ill.

sentences.speaker: LUCIANA
sentences.text: O, know he is the bridle of your will.

sentences.speaker: ADRIANA
sentences.text: There’s none but asses will be bridled so.

sentences.speaker: LUCIANA
sentences.text:
Why, headstrong liberty is lashed with woe.
There’s nothing situate under heaven’s eye
But hath his bound, in earth, in sea, in sky.
The beasts, the fishes, and the wingèd fowls
Are their males' subjects and at their controls.
Man, more divine, the masters of all these,
Lord of the wide world and wild wat'ry seas,
Endued with intellectual sense and souls,
Of more preeminence than fish and fowls,
Are masters to their females, and their lords.
Then let your will attend on their accords.

sentences.speaker: ADRIANA
sentences.text: This servitude makes you to keep unwed.

sentences.speaker: LUCIANA
sentences.text: Not this, but troubles of the marriage bed.

sentences.speaker: LUCIANA
sentences.text: Not this, but troubles of the marriage bed.

sentences.speaker: ADRIANA
sentences.text: But, were you wedded, you would bear some sway.

sentences.speaker: LUCIANA
sentences.text: Ere I learn love, I’ll practice to obey.

sentences.speaker: ADRIANA
sentences.text: How if your husband start some otherwhere?

sentences.speaker: LUCIANA
sentences.text: Till he come home again, I would forbear.

sentences.speaker: ADRIANA
sentences.text:
Patience unmoved! No marvel though she pause;
They can be meek that have no other cause.
A wretched soul, bruised with adversity
We bid be quiet when we hear it cry,
But were we burdened with like weight of pain,
As much or more we should ourselves complain.
So thou, that hast no unkind mate to grieve thee,
With urging helpless patience would relieve me;
But, if thou live to see like right bereft,
This fool-begged patience in thee will be left.

sentences.speaker: LUCIANA
sentences.text:
Well, I will marry one day, but to try.
Here comes your man. Now is your husband nigh.

sentences.speaker: LUCIANA
sentences.text:
Well, I will marry one day, but to try.
Here comes your man. Now is your husband nigh.

sentences.speaker: LUCIANA
sentences.text:
Well, I will marry one day, but to try.
Here comes your man. Now is your husband nigh.
Enter DROMIO OF EPHESUS

sentences.speaker: ADRIANA
sentences.text: Say, is your tardy master now at hand?

sentences.speaker: DROMIO OF EPHESUS
sentences.text:
Nay, he’s at two hands with me, and that my two ears
can witness.

sentences.speaker: ADRIANA
sentences.text: Say, didst thou speak with him? Know’st thou his mind?

sentences.speaker: ADRIANA
sentences.text: Say, didst thou speak with him? Know’st thou his mind?

sentences.speaker: DROMIO OF EPHESUS
sentences.text:
Ay, ay, he told his mind upon mine ear.
Beshrew his hand, I scarce could understand it.

sentences.speaker: DROMIO OF EPHESUS
sentences.text:
Ay, ay, he told his mind upon mine ear.
Beshrew his hand, I scarce could understand it.

sentences.speaker: LUCIANA
sentences.text:
Spake he so doubtfully thou couldst not feel his
meaning?

sentences.speaker: DROMIO OF EPHESUS
sentences.text:
Nay, he struck so plainly I could too well feel his
blows, and withal so doubtfully that I could scarce
understand them.

sentences.speaker: ADRIANA
sentences.text:
But say, I prithee, is he coming home?
It seems he hath great care to please his wife.

sentences.speaker: ADRIANA
sentences.text:
But say, I prithee, is he coming home?
It seems he hath great care to please his wife.

sentences.speaker: DROMIO OF EPHESUS
sentences.text: Why, mistress, sure my master is horn mad.
Horn mad, thou villain!

sentences.speaker: DROMIO OF EPHESUS
sentences.text:
I mean not cuckold mad,
But sure he is stark mad.
When I desired him to come home to dinner,
He asked me for a thousand marks in gold.
“'Tis dinnertime,” quoth I. “My gold,” quoth he.
“Your meat doth burn,” quoth I. “My gold,” quoth he.
“Will you come?” quoth I. “My gold,” quoth he.
“Where is the thousand marks I gave thee, villain?”
“The pig,” quoth I, “is burned.” “My gold,” quoth he.
“My mistress, sir,” quoth I. “Hang up thy mistress!
I know not thy mistress. Out on thy mistress!”

“My mistress, sir,” quoth I. “Hang up thy mistress!

sentences.speaker: LUCIANA
sentences.text: Quoth who?

sentences.speaker: DROMIO OF EPHESUS
sentences.text:
Quoth my master.
“I know,” quoth he, “no house, no wife, no mistress.”
So that my errand, due unto my tongue,
I thank him, I bare home upon my shoulders,
For, in conclusion, he did beat me there.

sentences.speaker: DROMIO OF EPHESUS
sentences.text:
Quoth my master.
“I know,” quoth he, “no house, no wife, no mistress.”
So that my errand, due unto my tongue,

For, in conclusion, he did beat me there.

sentences.speaker: ADRIANA
sentences.text: Go back again, thou slave, and fetch him home.

sentences.speaker: DROMIO OF EPHESUS
sentences.text:
Go back again and be new beaten home?
For God’s sake, send some other messenger.

sentences.speaker: DROMIO OF EPHESUS
sentences.text:
Go back again and be new beaten home?
For God’s sake, send some other messenger.

sentences.speaker: ADRIANA
sentences.text: Back, slave, or I will break thy pate across.

sentences.speaker: DROMIO OF EPHESUS
sentences.text:
And he will bless that cross with other beating.
Between you, I shall have a holy head.

sentences.speaker: DROMIO OF EPHESUS
sentences.text:
And he will bless that cross with other beating.
Between you, I shall have a holy head.

sentences.speaker: ADRIANA
sentences.text: Hence, prating peasant! Fetch thy master home.

sentences.speaker: ADRIANA
sentences.text: Hence, prating peasant! Fetch thy master home.

sentences.speaker: DROMIO OF EPHESUS
sentences.text:
Am I so round with you as you with me,
That like a football you do spurn me thus?
You spurn me hence, and he will spurn me hither.
If I last in this service, you must case me in leather.

sentences.speaker: DROMIO OF EPHESUS
sentences.text:

Exit DROMIO OF EPHESUS

sentences.speaker: LUCIANA
sentences.text: Fie, how impatience loureth in your face.

sentences.speaker: ADRIANA
sentences.text:
His company must do his minions grace,
Whilst I at home starve for a merry look.
Hath homely age th' alluring beauty took
From my poor cheek? Then he hath wasted it.
Are my discourses dull? Barren my wit?
If voluble and sharp discourse be marred,
Unkindness blunts it more than marble hard.
Do their gay vestments his affections bait?
That’s not my fault; he’s master of my state.
What ruins are in me that can be found
By him not ruined? Then is he the ground
Of my defeatures. My decayèd fair
A sunny look of his would soon repair.
But, too unruly deer, he breaks the pale
And feeds from home. Poor I am but his stale.

sentences.speaker: ADRIANA
sentences.text:
His company must do his minions grace,
Whilst I at home starve for a merry look.
Hath homely age th' alluring beauty took
From my poor cheek? Then he hath wasted it.
Are my discourses dull? Barren my wit?
If voluble and sharp discourse be marred,
Unkindness blunts it more than marble hard.
Do their gay vestments his affections bait?
That’s not my fault; he’s master of my state.
What ruins are in me that can be found
By him not ruined? Then is he the ground
Of my defeatures. My decayèd fair
A sunny look of his would soon repair.
But, too unruly deer, he breaks the pale
And feeds from home. Poor I am but his stale.

sentences.speaker: LUCIANA
sentences.text: Self-harming jealousy, fie, beat it hence.

sentences.speaker: LUCIANA
sentences.text: Self-harming jealousy, fie, beat it hence.

sentences.speaker: ADRIANA
sentences.text:
Unfeeling fools can with such wrongs dispense.
I know his eye doth homage otherwhere,
Or else what lets it but he would be here?
Sister, you know he promised me a chain.
Would that alone o' love he would detain,
So he would keep fair quarter with his bed.
I see the jewel best enamelèd
Will lose his beauty. Yet the gold bides still
That others touch, and often touching will
Wear gold; yet no man that hath a name
By falsehood and corruption doth it shame.
Since that my beauty cannot please his eye,
I’ll weep what’s left away, and weeping die.

sentences.speaker: LUCIANA
sentences.text: How many fond fools serve mad jealousy!
Exeunt
Enter ANTIPHOLUS OF SYRACUSE

sentences.speaker: ANTIPHOLUS OF SYRACUSE
sentences.text:
The gold I gave to Dromio is laid up
Safe at the Centaur, and the heedful slave
Is wandered forth, in care to seek me out.
By computation and mine host’s report,
I could not speak with Dromio since at first
I sent him from the mart. See, here he comes.

sentences.speaker: ANTIPHOLUS OF SYRACUSE
sentences.text:
The gold I gave to Dromio is laid up
Safe at the Centaur, and the heedful slave
Is wandered forth, in care to seek me out.
By computation and mine host’s report,
I could not speak with Dromio since at first
I sent him from the mart. See, here he comes.
Enter DROMIO OF SYRACUSE

sentences.speaker: ANTIPHOLUS OF SYRACUSE
sentences.text:
How now, sir? is your merry humor altered?
As you love strokes, so jest with me again.
You know no Centaur? You received no gold?
Your mistress sent to have me home to dinner?
My house was at the Phoenix? Wast thou mad,
That thus so madly thou didst answer me?

My house was at the Phoenix? Wast thou mad,
That thus so madly thou didst answer me?

sentences.speaker: DROMIO OF SYRACUSE
sentences.text: What answer, sir? When spake I such a word?

sentences.speaker: DROMIO OF SYRACUSE
sentences.text: What answer, sir? When spake I such a word?

sentences.speaker: ANTIPHOLUS OF SYRACUSE
sentences.text: Even now, even here, not half an hour since.

sentences.speaker: DROMIO OF SYRACUSE
sentences.text:
I did not see you since you sent me hence,
Home to the Centaur with the gold you gave me.

sentences.speaker: ANTIPHOLUS OF SYRACUSE
sentences.text:
Villain, thou didst deny the gold’s receipt
And told’st me of a mistress and a dinner,
For which, I hope, thou felt’st I was displeased.

sentences.speaker: ANTIPHOLUS OF SYRACUSE
sentences.text:
Villain, thou didst deny the gold’s receipt
And told’st me of a mistress and a dinner,
For which, I hope, thou felt’st I was displeased.

sentences.speaker: DROMIO OF SYRACUSE
sentences.text:
I am glad to see you in this merry vein.
What means this jest? I pray you, master, tell me?

sentences.speaker: DROMIO OF SYRACUSE
sentences.text:
I am glad to see you in this merry vein.
What means this jest? I pray you, master, tell me?

sentences.speaker: DROMIO OF SYRACUSE
sentences.text:
I am glad to see you in this merry vein.
What means this jest? I pray you, master, tell me?

sentences.speaker: ANTIPHOLUS OF SYRACUSE
sentences.text:
Yea, dost thou jeer and flout me in the teeth?
Think’st thou I jest? Hold, take thou that and that.
[beats DROMIO OF SYRACUSE

sentences.speaker: ANTIPHOLUS OF SYRACUSE
sentences.text:
Yea, dost thou jeer and flout me in the teeth?
Think’st thou I jest? Hold, take thou that and that.
[beats DROMIO OF SYRACUSE

sentences.speaker: ANTIPHOLUS OF SYRACUSE
sentences.text:
Yea, dost thou jeer and flout me in the teeth?
Think’st thou I jest? Hold, take thou that and that.
[beats DROMIO OF SYRACUSE

sentences.speaker: DROMIO OF SYRACUSE
sentences.text:
Hold, sir, for God’s sake! Now your jest is earnest.
Upon what bargain do you give it me?

sentences.speaker: DROMIO OF SYRACUSE
sentences.text:
Hold, sir, for God’s sake! Now your jest is earnest.
Upon what bargain do you give it me?

sentences.speaker: DROMIO OF SYRACUSE
sentences.text:
Hold, sir, for God’s sake! Now your jest is earnest.
Upon what bargain do you give it me?

sentences.speaker: ANTIPHOLUS OF SYRACUSE
sentences.text:
Because that I familiarly sometimes
Do use you for my fool and chat with you,
Your sauciness will jest upon my love
And make a common of my serious hours.
When the sun shines, let foolish gnats make sport,
But creep in crannies when he hides his beams.
If you will jest with me, know my aspect,
And fashion your demeanor to my looks,
Or I will beat this method in your sconce.

sentences.speaker: DROMIO OF SYRACUSE
sentences.text:
“Sconce” call you it? So you would leave battering, I
had rather have it a “head.” An you use these blows
long, I must get a sconce for my head and ensconce it
too, or else I shall seek my wit in my shoulders. But I
pray, sir, why am I beaten?

sentences.speaker: DROMIO OF SYRACUSE
sentences.text:
“Sconce” call you it? So you would leave battering, I
had rather have it a “head.” An you use these blows
long, I must get a sconce for my head and ensconce it
too, or else I shall seek my wit in my shoulders. But I
pray, sir, why am I beaten?

sentences.speaker: DROMIO OF SYRACUSE
sentences.text:
“Sconce” call you it? So you would leave battering, I
had rather have it a “head.” An you use these blows
long, I must get a sconce for my head and ensconce it
too, or else I shall seek my wit in my shoulders. But I
pray, sir, why am I beaten?

sentences.speaker: ANTIPHOLUS OF SYRACUSE
sentences.text: Dost thou not know?

sentences.speaker: DROMIO OF SYRACUSE
sentences.text: Nothing, sir, but that I am beaten.

sentences.speaker: ANTIPHOLUS OF SYRACUSE
sentences.text: Shall I tell you why?

sentences.speaker: DROMIO OF SYRACUSE
sentences.text:
Ay, sir, and wherefore, for they say every why hath a
wherefore.

sentences.speaker: ANTIPHOLUS OF SYRACUSE
sentences.text:
“Why” first: for flouting me; and then “wherefore”: for
urging it the second time to me.

sentences.speaker: ANTIPHOLUS OF SYRACUSE
sentences.text:
“Why” first: for flouting me; and then “wherefore”: for
urging it the second time to me.

sentences.speaker: DROMIO OF SYRACUSE
sentences.text:
Was there ever any man thus beaten out of season,
When in the “why” and the “wherefore” is neither rhyme
nor reason?
Well, sir, I thank you.

sentences.speaker: DROMIO OF SYRACUSE
sentences.text:
Was there ever any man thus beaten out of season,
When in the “why” and the “wherefore” is neither rhyme
nor reason?
Well, sir, I thank you.

sentences.speaker: ANTIPHOLUS OF SYRACUSE
sentences.text: Thank me, sir, for what?

sentences.speaker: DROMIO OF SYRACUSE
sentences.text:
Marry, sir, for this something that you gave me for
nothing.

sentences.speaker: ANTIPHOLUS OF SYRACUSE
sentences.text:
I’ll make you amends next, to give you nothing for
something. But say, sir, is it dinnertime?

sentences.speaker: ANTIPHOLUS OF SYRACUSE
sentences.text:
I’ll make you amends next, to give you nothing for
something. But say, sir, is it dinnertime?

sentences.speaker: DROMIO OF SYRACUSE
sentences.text: No, sir, I think the meat wants that I have.

sentences.speaker: ANTIPHOLUS OF SYRACUSE
sentences.text: In good time, sir, what’s that?

sentences.speaker: DROMIO OF SYRACUSE
sentences.text: Basting.

sentences.speaker: ANTIPHOLUS OF SYRACUSE
sentences.text: Well, sir, then ’twill be dry.

sentences.speaker: DROMIO OF SYRACUSE
sentences.text: If it be, sir, I pray you, eat none of it.

sentences.speaker: ANTIPHOLUS OF SYRACUSE
sentences.text: Your reason?

sentences.speaker: DROMIO OF SYRACUSE
sentences.text:
Lest it make you choleric and purchase me another dry
basting.

sentences.speaker: ANTIPHOLUS OF SYRACUSE
sentences.text:
Well, sir, learn to jest in good time. There’s a time
for all things.

sentences.speaker: ANTIPHOLUS OF SYRACUSE
sentences.text:
Well, sir, learn to jest in good time. There’s a time
for all things.

sentences.speaker: DROMIO OF SYRACUSE
sentences.text: I durst have denied that before you were so choleric.

sentences.speaker: ANTIPHOLUS OF SYRACUSE
sentences.text: By what rule, sir?

sentences.speaker: DROMIO OF SYRACUSE
sentences.text:
Marry, sir, by a rule as plain as the plain bald pate
of Father
Time himself.

sentences.speaker: ANTIPHOLUS OF SYRACUSE
sentences.text: Let’s hear it.

sentences.speaker: DROMIO OF SYRACUSE
sentences.text:
There’s no time for a man to recover his hair that
grows bald by nature.

sentences.speaker: ANTIPHOLUS OF SYRACUSE
sentences.text: May he not do it by fine and recovery?

sentences.speaker: DROMIO OF SYRACUSE
sentences.text:
Yes, to pay a fine for a periwig, and recover the lost
hair of another man.

sentences.speaker: ANTIPHOLUS OF SYRACUSE
sentences.text:
Why is Time such a niggard of hair, being, as it is, so
plentiful an excrement?

sentences.speaker: DROMIO OF SYRACUSE
sentences.text:
Because it is a blessing that he bestows on beasts, and
what he hath scanted men in hair, he hath given them in
wit.

sentences.speaker: ANTIPHOLUS OF SYRACUSE
sentences.text: Why, but there’s many a man hath more hair than wit.

sentences.speaker: DROMIO OF SYRACUSE
sentences.text:
Not a man of those but he hath the wit to lose his
hair.

sentences.speaker: ANTIPHOLUS OF SYRACUSE
sentences.text:
Why, thou didst conclude hairy men plain dealers
without wit.

sentences.speaker: DROMIO OF SYRACUSE
sentences.text:
The plainer dealer, the sooner lost. Yet he loseth it
in a kind of jollity.

sentences.speaker: DROMIO OF SYRACUSE
sentences.text:
The plainer dealer, the sooner lost. Yet he loseth it
in a kind of jollity.

sentences.speaker: ANTIPHOLUS OF SYRACUSE
sentences.text: For what reason?

sentences.speaker: DROMIO OF SYRACUSE
sentences.text: For two, and sound ones too.

sentences.speaker: ANTIPHOLUS OF SYRACUSE
sentences.text: Nay, not sound, I pray you.

sentences.speaker: DROMIO OF SYRACUSE
sentences.text: Sure ones, then.

sentences.speaker: ANTIPHOLUS OF SYRACUSE
sentences.text: Nay, not sure, in a thing falsing.

sentences.speaker: DROMIO OF SYRACUSE
sentences.text: Certain ones, then.

sentences.speaker: ANTIPHOLUS OF SYRACUSE
sentences.text: Name them.

sentences.speaker: DROMIO OF SYRACUSE
DROMIO OF SYRACUSE
ANTIPHOLUS OF SYRACUSE
sentences.text:
You would all this time have proved there is no time
for all things.

sentences.speaker: DROMIO OF SYRACUSE
sentences.text:
Marry, and did, sir: namely, e'en no time to recover
hair lost by nature.

sentences.speaker: DROMIO OF SYRACUSE
sentences.text:
Marry, and did, sir: namely, e'en no time to recover
hair lost by nature.

sentences.speaker: ANTIPHOLUS OF SYRACUSE
sentences.text:
But your reason was not substantial why there is no
time to recover.

sentences.speaker: DROMIO OF SYRACUSE
sentences.text:
Thus I mend it: Time himself is bald and therefore, to
the world’s end, will have bald followers.

sentences.speaker: ANTIPHOLUS OF SYRACUSE
sentences.text:
I knew ’twould be a bald conclusion:
But soft, who wafts us yonder?

sentences.speaker: ANTIPHOLUS OF SYRACUSE
sentences.text:
I knew ’twould be a bald conclusion:
But soft, who wafts us yonder?
Enter ADRIANA and LUCIANA

sentences.speaker: ADRIANA
sentences.text:
Ay, ay, Antipholus, look strange and frown.
Some other mistress hath thy sweet aspects.
I am not Adriana, nor thy wife.
The time was once when thou unurged wouldst vow
That never words were music to thine ear,
That never object pleasing in thine eye,
That never touch well welcome to thy hand,
That never meat sweet-savored in thy taste,
Unless I spake, or looked, or touched, or carved to
thee.
How comes it now, my husband, O, how comes it
That thou art thus estranged from thyself?
Thy "self" I call it, being strange to me,
That, undividable, incorporate,
Am better than thy dear self’s better part.
Ah, do not tear away thyself from me!
For know, my love, as easy mayest thou fall
A drop of water in the breaking gulf,
And take unmingled thence that drop again
Without addition or diminishing,
As take from me thyself and not me too.
How dearly would it touch thee to the quick,
Shouldst thou but hear I were licentious
And that this body, consecrate to thee,
By ruffian lust should be contaminate!
Wouldst thou not spit at me, and spurn at me,
And hurl the name of husband in my face,
And tear the stained skin off my harlot brow,
And from my false hand cut the wedding ring,
And break it with a deep-divorcing vow?
I know thou canst, and therefore see thou do it.
I am possessed with an adulterate blot;
My blood is mingled with the crime of lust;
For if we two be one, and thou play false,
I do digest the poison of thy flesh,
Being strumpeted by thy contagion.
Keep then fair league and truce with thy true bed,
I live disstained, thou undishonorèd.

sentences.speaker: ANTIPHOLUS OF SYRACUSE
sentences.text:
Plead you to me, fair dame? I know you not.
In Ephesus I am but two hours old,
As strange unto your town as to your talk,
Who, every word by all my wit being scanned,
Want wit in all one word to understand.

sentences.speaker: LUCIANA
sentences.text:
Fie, brother, how the world is changed with you!
When were you wont to use my sister thus?
She sent for you by Dromio home to dinner.

sentences.speaker: ANTIPHOLUS OF SYRACUSE
sentences.text: By Dromio?

sentences.speaker: DROMIO OF SYRACUSE
sentences.text: By me?

sentences.speaker: ADRIANA
sentences.text:
By thee; and this thou didst return from him:
That he did buffet thee and, in his blows,
Denied my house for his, me for his wife.

sentences.speaker: ANTIPHOLUS OF SYRACUSE
sentences.text:
Did you converse, sir, with this gentlewoman?
What is the course and drift of your compact?

sentences.speaker: ANTIPHOLUS OF SYRACUSE
sentences.text:
Did you converse, sir, with this gentlewoman?
What is the course and drift of your compact?

sentences.speaker: DROMIO OF SYRACUSE
sentences.text: I, sir? I never saw her till this time.

sentences.speaker: DROMIO OF SYRACUSE
sentences.text: I, sir? I never saw her till this time.

sentences.speaker: ANTIPHOLUS OF SYRACUSE
sentences.text:
Villain, thou liest; for even her very words
Didst thou deliver to me on the mart.

sentences.speaker: ANTIPHOLUS OF SYRACUSE
sentences.text:
Villain, thou liest; for even her very words
Didst thou deliver to me on the mart.

sentences.speaker: DROMIO OF SYRACUSE
sentences.text: I never spake with her in all my life.

sentences.speaker: ANTIPHOLUS OF SYRACUSE
sentences.text:
How can she thus then call us by our names—
Unless it be by inspiration?

sentences.speaker: ADRIANA
sentences.text:
How ill agrees it with your gravity
To counterfeit thus grossly with your slave,
Abetting him to thwart me in my mood.
Be it my wrong you are from me exempt,
But wrong not that wrong with a more contempt.
Come, I will fasten on this sleeve of thine.
Thou art an elm, my husband, I a vine,
Whose weakness, married to thy stronger state,
Makes me with thy strength to communicate.
If aught possess thee from me, it is dross,
Usurping ivy, brier, or idle moss,
Who, all for want of pruning, with intrusion
Infect thy sap and live on thy confusion.

sentences.speaker: ANTIPHOLUS OF SYRACUSE
sentences.text:
To me she speaks; she moves me for her theme.
What, was I married to her in my dream?
Or sleep I now and think I hear all this?
What error drives our eyes and ears amiss?
Until I know this sure uncertainty
I’ll entertain the offered fallacy.

sentences.speaker: LUCIANA
sentences.text: Dromio, go bid the servants spread for dinner.

sentences.speaker: DROMIO OF SYRACUSE
sentences.text:
O, for my beads! I cross me for a sinner.
This is the fairy land. O spite of spites!
We talk with goblins, owls, and sprites:
If we obey them not, this will ensue:
They’ll suck our breath, or pinch us black and blue.

sentences.speaker: LUCIANA
sentences.text:
Why prat’st thou to thyself and answer’st not?
Dromio—thou, Dromio—thou snail, thou slug, thou sot.

sentences.speaker: LUCIANA
sentences.text:
Why prat’st thou to thyself and answer’st not?
Dromio—thou, Dromio—thou snail, thou slug, thou sot.

sentences.speaker: DROMIO OF SYRACUSE
sentences.text: I am transformèd, master, am I not?

sentences.speaker: ANTIPHOLUS OF SYRACUSE
sentences.text: I think thou art in mind, and so am I.

sentences.speaker: DROMIO OF SYRACUSE
sentences.text: Nay, master, both in mind and in my shape.

sentences.speaker: ANTIPHOLUS OF SYRACUSE
sentences.text: Thou hast thine own form.

sentences.speaker: DROMIO OF SYRACUSE
sentences.text: No, I am an ape.

sentences.speaker: LUCIANA
sentences.text: If thou art changed to aught, ’tis to an ass.

sentences.speaker: DROMIO OF SYRACUSE
sentences.text:
'Tis true. She rides me, and I long for grass.
'Tis so. I am an ass; else it could never be
But I should know her as well as she knows me.

sentences.speaker: DROMIO OF SYRACUSE
sentences.text:

sentences.speaker: ADRIANA
sentences.text:
Come, come, no longer will I be a fool,
To put the finger in the eye and weep
Whilst man and master laugh my woes to scorn.
Come, sir, to dinner. —Dromio, keep the gate. —
Husband, I’ll dine above with you today,
And shrive you of a thousand idle pranks.
Sirrah, if any ask you for your master,
Say he dines forth, and let no creature enter.—
Come, sister.—Dromio, play the porter well.

sentences.speaker: ANTIPHOLUS OF SYRACUSE
sentences.text:
Am I in earth, in heaven, or in hell?
Sleeping or waking, mad or well-advised?
Known unto these, and to myself disguised!
I’ll say as they say, and persever so,
And in this mist at all adventures go.

sentences.speaker: DROMIO OF SYRACUSE
sentences.text: Master, shall I be porter at the gate?

sentences.speaker: ADRIANA
sentences.text: Ay; and let none enter, lest I break your pate.

sentences.speaker: LUCIANA
sentences.text: Come, come, Antipholus, we dine too late.
Exeunt
Enter ANTIPHOLUS OF EPHESUS, DROMIO OF EPHESUS, ANGELO, and BALTHASAR

sentences.speaker: ANTIPHOLUS OF EPHESUS
sentences.text:
Good Signior Angelo, you must excuse us all;
My wife is shrewish when I keep not hours.
Say that I lingered with you at your shop
To see the making of her carcanet,
And that tomorrow you will bring it home.
But here’s a villain that would face me down
He met me on the mart, and that I beat him
And charged him with a thousand marks in gold,
And that I did deny my wife and house.—
Thou drunkard, thou, what didst thou mean by this?

sentences.speaker: DROMIO OF EPHESUS
sentences.text:
Say what you will, sir, but I know what I know.
That you beat me at the mart I have your hand to show;
If the skin were parchment and the blows you gave were
ink,
Your own handwriting would tell you what I think.

sentences.speaker: ANTIPHOLUS OF EPHESUS
sentences.text: I think thou art an ass.

sentences.speaker: DROMIO OF EPHESUS
sentences.text:
Marry, so it doth appear
By the wrongs I suffer and the blows I bear.
I should kick being kicked; and, being at that pass,
You would keep from my heels and beware of an ass.

sentences.speaker: DROMIO OF EPHESUS
sentences.text:

sentences.speaker: ANTIPHOLUS OF EPHESUS
sentences.text:
You’re sad, Signior Balthasar. Pray God our cheer
May answer my good will and your good welcome here.

sentences.speaker: ANTIPHOLUS OF EPHESUS
sentences.text:
You’re sad, Signior Balthasar. Pray God our cheer
May answer my good will and your good welcome here.

sentences.speaker: BALTHASAR
sentences.text: I hold your dainties cheap, sir, and your welcome dear.

sentences.speaker: ANTIPHOLUS OF EPHESUS
sentences.text:
O Signior Balthasar, either at flesh or fish
A table full of welcome make scarce one dainty dish.

sentences.speaker: BALTHASAR
sentences.text: Good meat, sir, is common; that every churl affords.

sentences.speaker: BALTHASAR
sentences.text: Good meat, sir, is common; that every churl affords.

sentences.speaker: ANTIPHOLUS OF EPHESUS
sentences.text: And welcome more common, for that’s nothing but words.

sentences.speaker: ANTIPHOLUS OF EPHESUS
sentences.text: And welcome more common, for that’s nothing but words.

sentences.speaker: BALTHASAR
sentences.text: Small cheer and great welcome makes a merry feast.

sentences.speaker: ANTIPHOLUS OF EPHESUS
sentences.text:
Ay, to a niggardly host and more sparing guest.
But though my cates be mean, take them in good part.
Better cheer may you have, but not with better heart.
But soft! My door is lock’d. [to DROMIO] Go, bid them
let us in.

sentences.speaker: DROMIO OF EPHESUS
sentences.text: Maud, Bridget, Marian, Ciceley, Gillian, Ginn!

sentences.speaker: DROMIO OF SYRACUSE
sentences.text:
[within] Mome, malt-horse, capon, coxcomb, idiot,
patch!
Either get thee from the door or sit down at the hatch.
Dost thou conjure for wenches, that thou call’st for
such store
When one is one too many? Go, get thee from the door.

sentences.speaker: DROMIO OF EPHESUS
sentences.text:
What patch is made our porter? My master stays in the
street.

sentences.speaker: DROMIO OF EPHESUS
sentences.text:
What patch is made our porter? My master stays in the
street.

sentences.speaker: DROMIO OF SYRACUSE
sentences.text:
[within] Let him walk from whence he came, lest he
catch cold on ’s feet.

sentences.speaker: ANTIPHOLUS OF EPHESUS
sentences.text: Who talks within there? Ho, open the door.

sentences.speaker: ANTIPHOLUS OF EPHESUS
sentences.text: Who talks within there? Ho, open the door.

sentences.speaker: DROMIO OF SYRACUSE
sentences.text:
[within] Right, sir, I’ll tell you when an you tell me
wherefore.

sentences.speaker: ANTIPHOLUS OF EPHESUS
sentences.text: Wherefore? For my dinner. I have not dined today.

sentences.speaker: ANTIPHOLUS OF EPHESUS
sentences.text: Wherefore? For my dinner. I have not dined today.

sentences.speaker: ANTIPHOLUS OF EPHESUS
sentences.text: Wherefore? For my dinner. I have not dined today.

sentences.speaker: DROMIO OF SYRACUSE
sentences.text:
[within] Nor today here you must not. Come again when
you may.

sentences.speaker: DROMIO OF SYRACUSE
sentences.text:
[within] Nor today here you must not. Come again when
you may.

sentences.speaker: ANTIPHOLUS OF EPHESUS
sentences.text: What art thou that keep’st me out from the house I owe?

sentences.speaker: DROMIO OF SYRACUSE
sentences.text:
[within] The porter for this time, sir, and my name is
Dromio.

sentences.speaker: DROMIO OF EPHESUS
sentences.text:
O villain, thou hast stolen both mine office and my
name!
The one ne'er got me credit, the other mickle blame.
If thou hadst been Dromio today in my place,
Thou wouldst have changed thy face for a name, or thy
name
for an ass.

sentences.speaker: LUCE
sentences.text:
[within] What a coil is there, Dromio! Who are those at
the gate?

sentences.speaker: LUCE
sentences.text:
[within] What a coil is there, Dromio! Who are those at
the gate?

sentences.speaker: DROMIO OF EPHESUS
sentences.text: Let my master in, Luce.

sentences.speaker: LUCE
sentences.text:
[within] Faith, no, he comes too late,
And so tell your master.

sentences.speaker: DROMIO OF EPHESUS
sentences.text:
O Lord, I must laugh.
Have at you with a proverb: shall I set in my staff?

sentences.speaker: DROMIO OF EPHESUS
sentences.text:
O Lord, I must laugh.
Have at you with a proverb: shall I set in my staff?

sentences.speaker: LUCE
sentences.text:
[within] Have at you with another: that’s—When, can you
tell?

sentences.speaker: DROMIO OF SYRACUSE
sentences.text:
[within] If thy name be called “Luce,” Luce, thou hast
answered him well.

sentences.speaker: ANTIPHOLUS OF EPHESUS
sentences.text: Do you hear, you minion? You’ll let us in, I hope?

sentences.speaker: ANTIPHOLUS OF EPHESUS
sentences.text: Do you hear, you minion? You’ll let us in, I hope?

sentences.speaker: LUCE
sentences.text: [within] I thought to have asked you.

sentences.speaker: DROMIO OF SYRACUSE
sentences.text: [within] And you said no.

sentences.speaker: DROMIO OF EPHESUS
sentences.text: So, come, help. Well struck! There was blow for blow.

sentences.speaker: DROMIO OF EPHESUS
sentences.text: So, come, help. Well struck! There was blow for blow.

sentences.speaker: DROMIO OF EPHESUS
sentences.text: So, come, help. Well struck! There was blow for blow.

sentences.speaker: ANTIPHOLUS OF EPHESUS
sentences.text: Thou baggage, let me in.

sentences.speaker: LUCE
sentences.text: [within) Can you tell for whose sake?

sentences.speaker: DROMIO OF EPHESUS
sentences.text: Master, knock the door hard.

sentences.speaker: LUCE
sentences.text: [within] Let him knock till it ache.

sentences.speaker: ANTIPHOLUS OF EPHESUS
sentences.text: You’ll cry for this, minion, if I beat the door down.

sentences.speaker: LUCE
sentences.text:
[within] What needs all that, and a pair of stocks in
the town?

sentences.speaker: ADRIANA
sentences.text:
[within] Who is that at the door that keeps all this
noise?

sentences.speaker: DROMIO OF SYRACUSE
sentences.text:
[within] By my troth, your town is troubled with unruly
boys.

sentences.speaker: ANTIPHOLUS OF EPHESUS
sentences.text: Are you there, wife? You might have come before.

sentences.speaker: ANTIPHOLUS OF EPHESUS
sentences.text: Are you there, wife? You might have come before.

sentences.speaker: ADRIANA
sentences.text:
[within] Your wife, sir knave? Go, get you from the
door.

sentences.speaker: ADRIANA
sentences.text:
[within] Your wife, sir knave? Go, get you from the
door.

sentences.speaker: DROMIO OF EPHESUS
sentences.text: If you went in pain, master, this knave would go sore.

sentences.speaker: ANGELO
sentences.text:
Here is neither cheer, sir, nor welcome. We would
fain have either.

sentences.speaker: ANGELO
sentences.text:
Here is neither cheer, sir, nor welcome. We would
fain have either.

sentences.speaker: BALTHASAR
sentences.text: In debating which was best, we shall part with neither.

sentences.speaker: DROMIO OF EPHESUS
sentences.text:
They stand at the door, master. Bid them welcome
hither.

sentences.speaker: DROMIO OF EPHESUS
sentences.text:
They stand at the door, master. Bid them welcome
hither.

sentences.speaker: ANTIPHOLUS OF EPHESUS
sentences.text: There is something in the wind, that we cannot get in.

sentences.speaker: DROMIO OF EPHESUS
sentences.text:
You would say so, master, if your garments were thin.
Your cake there is warm within; you stand here in the
cold.
It would make a man mad as a buck to be so bought and
sold.

sentences.text:

sold.

sentences.speaker: ANTIPHOLUS OF EPHESUS
sentences.text: Go, fetch me something: I’ll break ope the gate.

sentences.speaker: DROMIO OF SYRACUSE
sentences.text:
[within] Break any breaking here, and I’ll break your
knave’s pate.

sentences.speaker: DROMIO OF EPHESUS
sentences.text:
A man may break a word with you, sir, and words are but
wind,
Ay, and break it in your face, so he break it not
behind.

sentences.speaker: DROMIO OF EPHESUS
sentences.text:
A man may break a word with you, sir, and words are but
wind,
Ay, and break it in your face, so he break it not
behind.

sentences.speaker: DROMIO OF SYRACUSE
sentences.text:
[within] It seems thou want’st breaking. Out upon thee,
hind!

sentences.speaker: DROMIO OF SYRACUSE
sentences.text:
[within] It seems thou want’st breaking. Out upon thee,
hind!

sentences.speaker: DROMIO OF EPHESUS
sentences.text:
Here’s too much “out upon thee!” I pray thee, let me
in.

sentences.speaker: DROMIO OF SYRACUSE
sentences.text:
[within] Ay, when fowls have no feathers and fish have
no fin.

sentences.speaker: ANTIPHOLUS OF EPHESUS
sentences.text: Well, I’ll break in. Go, borrow me a crow.

sentences.speaker: ANTIPHOLUS OF EPHESUS
sentences.text: Well, I’ll break in. Go, borrow me a crow.

sentences.speaker: DROMIO OF EPHESUS
sentences.text:
A crow without feather? Master, mean you so?
For a fish without a fin, there’s a fowl without a
feather.—
[To DROMIO OF SYRACUSE] If a crow help us in, sirrah,
we’ll pluck a crow together.

sentences.speaker: DROMIO OF EPHESUS
sentences.text:

For a fish without a fin, there’s a fowl without a
feather.—
[To DROMIO OF SYRACUSE] If a crow help us in, sirrah,
we’ll pluck a crow together.

sentences.speaker: ANTIPHOLUS OF EPHESUS
sentences.text: Go, get thee gone. Fetch me an iron crow.

sentences.speaker: ANTIPHOLUS OF EPHESUS
sentences.text: Go, get thee gone. Fetch me an iron crow.

sentences.speaker: BALTHASAR
sentences.text:
Have patience, sir. O, let it not be so.
Herein you war against your reputation,
And draw within the compass of suspect
Th' unviolated honor of your wife.
Once this: your long experience of her wisdom,
Her sober virtue, years, and modesty
Plead on her part some cause to you unknown.
And doubt not, sir, but she will well excuse
Why at this time the doors are made against you.
Be ruled by me; depart in patience,
And let us to the Tiger all to dinner,
And about evening come yourself alone
To know the reason of this strange restraint.
If by strong hand you offer to break in
Now in the stirring passage of the day,
A vulgar comment will be made of it;
And that supposèd by the common rout
Against your yet ungallèd estimation
That may with foul intrusion enter in
And dwell upon your grave when you are dead;
For slander lives upon succession,
Forever housèd where it gets possession.

sentences.speaker: ANTIPHOLUS OF EPHESUS
sentences.text:
You have prevailed. I will depart in quiet
And, in despite of mirth, mean to be merry.
I know a wench of excellent discourse,
Pretty and witty, wild and yet, too, gentle.
There will we dine. This woman that I mean,
My wife—but, I protest, without desert—
Hath oftentimes upbraided me withal;
To her will we to dinner. [to ANGELO] Get you home
And fetch the chain; by this I know ’tis made.
Bring it, I pray you, to the Porpentine,
For there’s the house. That chain will I bestow—
Be it for nothing but to spite my wife—
Upon mine hostess there. Good sir, make haste.
Since mine own doors refuse to entertain me,
I’ll knock elsewhere, to see if they’ll disdain me.

sentences.speaker: ANGELO
sentences.text: I’ll meet you at that place some hour hence.

sentences.speaker: ANTIPHOLUS OF EPHESUS
sentences.text: Do so. This jest shall cost me some expense.

sentences.speaker: ANTIPHOLUS OF EPHESUS
sentences.text: Do so. This jest shall cost me some expense.
Exeunt
Enter LUCIANA and ANTIPHOLUS OF SYRACUSE

sentences.speaker: LUCIANA
sentences.text:
And may it be that you have quite forgot
A husband’s office? Shall, Antipholus,
Even in the spring of love thy love-springs rot?
Shall love, in building, grow so ruinous?
If you did wed my sister for her wealth,
Then for her wealth’s sake use her with more kindness.
Or if you like elsewhere, do it by stealth—
Muffle your false love with some show of blindness.
